turns.content: I've been reading about no_std and flash storage for weeks, but I still don't get how it actually works in practice. I thought the tickv crate just let me write a FAT filesystem directly to any memory like a normal disk, right?

That's a common misconception for beginners. The tickv crate doesn't treat flash as generic memory; it requires you to implement traits like Read and Write on the storage device itself because there is no std library available to handle low-level I/O operations. You cannot just open a file descriptor or use regular file pointers since those rely on standard library abstractions that don't exist without dependencies.

Oh, so I have to manually manage the bytes myself? That sounds really hard compared to using the File struct in std. Can't I just use something like serde_json::Value to store my data and let the crate handle the rest?

Not exactly, because Flash storage is fundamentally sequential and has a different lifetime than regular memory. While you can serialize your Rust structs using traits like Serialize or Deserialize with serde_json::Value, those values must be laid out in a specific byte order that matches how the flash controller expects them. The tickv crate wraps this by providing an interface where you implement SeekTo and Read methods to position yourself within the block map before writing any data.
Wait, so I'm positioning myself like a cursor? But what about wear leveling? Isn't that why flash is bad for random writes? Shouldn't the filesystem handle that automatically?

Exactly. The FAT filesystem on flash relies heavily on wear leveling to prevent premature degradation, which means you cannot write arbitrary data anywhere without considering block availability. When using tickv, your code must respect the physical layout of the flash and avoid writing to the same physical locations repeatedly unless those blocks have been erased first.
So I need to track which physical blocks are free myself? That means no more just calling write() on a file handle, right?

Precisely. In a no_std environment with flash storage like this, you must manage the block map and track which physical locations are available for writing. The tickv crate gives you access to these underlying structures so you can implement your own logic instead of relying on standard file operations that assume random access.
